9 min read
Just now

--

This blog post summarizes our work “Do Unit Proofs Work? An Empirical Study of Compositional Bounded Model Checking for Memory Safety Verification”, which appeared at the 2026 International Conference on Software Engineering (ICSE’26). The published paper is here.
This post is by Paschal C. Amusuo, with light editing by me.

Background: Memory safety defects
Remember the CrowdStrike outage in 2024? Millions of computer systems went down. Flights were delayed or cancelled. Hospitals switched over to emergency backups. Businesses were disrupted. Microsoft estimated that 8.5 million Windows systems were affected globally. The total cost was estimated at approximately $10 billion.
According to CrowdStrike’s root-cause analysis, the outage was caused by a memory-safety bug: an out-of-bounds memory read. These bugs remain one of the most persistent causes of both software failures and cybersecurity attacks. Google’s Project Zero analysis showed that they account for over half of the zero-day cybersecurity exploits in the wild.
At a high level, memory-safety bugs happen when software reads from or writes to memory it should not access. They can lead to a system crash, data leak, privilege escalation, or remote code execution. Because of how critical and prevalent these bugs are, the government, industry, and academia have all identified it as a priority, advocating for and investing heavily in techniques that prevent, detect, or defend against them.
If you’re starting a new project, you might opt for a memory-safe language, like Rust. But what if you have an existing C or C++ system, and rewriting it in Rust is unrealistic because of certification, legacy dependencies, toolchain constraints, or sheer engineering cost? That leaves you with a problem. Most bug finding techniques are best effort. For example, static analysis and fuzzing can find many important bugs but they cannot tell you when code is truly memory safe. On the other end of the spectrum of guarantees, formal verification techniques can prove memory safety but are often considered too expensive for real-world software projects. What to do, what to do?
Unit proofing
Unit Proofing aims to bridge this gap.
Definition
The idea is simple: instead of verifying an entire system at once, engineers verify one function or component at a time. They write small verification harnesses that look and feel like unit tests. But instead of checking one concrete input, these harnesses allow a verifier to reason over many possible executions. We call these harnesses Unit Proofs, and the technique, Unit Proofing.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Unit Proofing: A scoped application of formal methods that lets you prove the absence of memory safety defects within the modeled environment.Under the hood, Unit Proofing uses Bounded Model Checking (BMC). BMC converts the program and the properties we want to check into a mathematical formula. A constraint solver then searches for an execution that violates the property. If it finds one, we get a counterexample. If it does not, we get a proof that the property holds within the bounds and assumptions used during verification.
BMC tools such as CBMC and Kani can automatically add memory-safety properties to check the validity of memory read and write operations. On small programs, they can find these defects almost for “free”. On large programs, solvers struggle, and engineers have to bound loop iterations, limit input sizes and exclude some functions from the analysis. Engineers also need to define assumptions about their behaviors and variable states. These choices make verification tractable, but they also determine the guarantees the proof provides.
I hope this process sounds familiar — it’s the same kind of decision-making sequence you go through when you write unit tests for a component. Some tests are too costly without shortcuts or mocks, and the validity of those choices affects the interpretability of a “tests are green” result. Several industry reports, including from Amazon, ARM and Ant Group, have reported using this unit-test-like approach to verify components in critical software systems such as embedded operating systems and confidential computing systems.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Left — source program we wish to validate. Right — example unit proof. Note the call to _unwind, which dictates the number of loop unwindings during the model checking run. The harness also models the environment, e.g., that there is not memory pressure and hence malloc returns non-NULL.A unit proof is much stronger than a test case. It is an executable proof obligation: the verifier explores the possible values consistent with the assumptions and either finds a memory-safety violation or proves that none exists within the configured bounds.
State of the art
There is no standardized or principled way to write Unit Proofs.
Different organizations use different methods. Different engineers make different modeling choices. If the harness is wrong, the proof can be misleading. If the assumptions are too strong, the verifier may miss real bugs. If the bounds are too small, the proof may not cover buggy behavior. If the assumptions are too weak or the bounds are too large, verification may not finish.
For example, imagine a bug that only appears after 10 loop iterations. A harness that checks only 5 iterations will miss it. A harness that checks 20 iterations may be too expensive to verify. The challenge is finding a useful middle ground.
In our paper, we ask whether Unit Proofing can be made more systematic.
Systematizing Unit Proofing: A Bottom-Up Feedback-Driven Approach
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Study methodology. We first develop unit proofs through an iterative five-step process, refining them until we achieve coverage and eliminate reported memory-safety violations within the modeled bounds. We then evaluate whether the resulting proofs expose known vulnerabilities and what they cost to develop and run.The method
The key challenge in creating unit proofs is identifying bounds and model assumptions such that the resulting verification exposes all and only real defects. That goal is probably impossible, so we’ll settle for something weaker: identifying bounds and model assumptions that expose real defects without overwhelming the engineers with infeasible or overly broad verification tasks. Prior industry reports adopt a top-down model where bounds and models are defined based on expected behaviors, project-level expertise, developer intuition and existing specifications, and then used to check the target component. However, this approach makes creation of unit proofs costly and error-prone.
Instead of relying only on developer intuition or project expertise, we ask if a more systematic methodology can be used to bootstrap unit proofs. We specifically investigated a bottom-up, feedback-driven approach. Our goal was not to dictate how EVERYONE should do this, but rather to describe and evaluate a specific method that SOMEONE might find helpful.

• Our method starts with the target component. We make a Starter Unit Proof, with single loop bounds and mostly unconstrained models.

• Then, we use assume-guarantee reasoning: first identify the assumptions under which the component is memory safe, and then check whether those assumptions hold in the broader program. We refine it using three kinds of verification feedback. Timeouts tell us when the verification problem is too large. Line coverage reports tell us what important code paths are not being exercised. Property violations tell us when the verifier found a potential memory-safety error.

• Based on this feedback, we apply targeted interventions. We may stub out a callee to address a timeout. We may increase a loop bound to improve coverage. We may infer the weakest input or function-model assumption needed to eliminate a property violation. We build and use a catalog of interventions. We repeat this process until we no longer identify refinement opportunities.
The result is a Systematic Unit Proof. It records the conditions under which the bounded component is memory safe, with loop bounds chosen to expose memory-safety violations that occur during the first execution of each covered line. This is not an absolute guarantee — some safety errors require repeated executions of the same line and would not be exposed. However, it gives engineers a concrete, reviewable starting point that can be strengthened with domain expertise. (And, since it’s 2026, having a playbook gives us a target for agents — see our follow-up paper).
The measurement
All the preceding sounds good in theory, and we tried it out for ourselves as we developed the method. But I’m sure you want some data: If we develop unit proofs using this uniform and systematic approach, would they expose known memory-safety vulnerabilities? If so, what do they cost?
We studied four embedded operating systems, FreeRTOS, Zephyr RTOS, RIOT, and Contiki-NG and focused on security vulnerabilities because they are critical bugs that lead to exploitation. We selected 89 known memory-safety vulnerabilities across these systems. We identified the functions affected by the defects, and then asked two undergraduate students to create unit proofs for those functions using our systematic methodology. Importantly, they did not know what the vulnerabilities were.
After the unit proofs were created, a different author recreated the vulnerabilities by removing their fixes. We then checked whether the unit proofs exposed the bugs. In total, we developed 73 unit proofs across the four operating systems.
Finding 1: Unit proofing exposes known defects plus new ones
The first result was encouraging. Systematically created unit proofs exposed 66 of the 89 known defects (74%) without any modification. An additional 8 defects, or 9%, were exposed after increasing program or loop bounds. This gap highlights the opportunity for expert intervention and motivates future research.
So in total, unit proofing exposed 83% of the known memory-safety defects in our study. The main reason some defects were not exposed was because the verifier ran out of resources during verification, which could plausibly be addressed by money if you’re a company instead of a shoestring university research lab.
Along the way, the unit proofs also exposed 20 new vulnerabilities in the verified functions. Eleven of these were assigned CVEs. So unit proofing did not only rediscover old bugs, it also found new ones within the previously vulnerable functions.
Table showing the proportion of known vulnerabilities that were exposed by unit proofing, and the number of new vulnerabilities found.Finding 2: The proofs were small
One concern with formal verification is that the proof effort can become larger and more complicated than the code being verified. That was not what we observed, thanks to the small scope we pursued for verification.
The unit proofs in our study averaged about 20 lines of code. Most were under 50 lines. On average, they modeled 5 variables and 2 functions. Most of the modeling was simple. About 76% of modeled variables were assumptions about pointer nullity or allocation sizes. These are the kinds of assumptions that can be inferred statically using dataflow analysis.
Finding 3: Unit proofs were cheap to develop and run
The median unit proof took 72 minutes to develop, completed verification in 25 seconds, and verified 185 lines of code.
These numbers suggest that Unit Proofing can fit within normal software engineering workflows. Engineers could write Unit Proofs for critical functions added or modified during a sprint, and the proofs could run in CI/CD pipelines.
Finding 4: A systematic process beats ad hoc expertise
To put the preceding claim in context, Amazon has reported that two engineers took about one month to verify 1,500 lines of code. Extr
